pattern_area: Microcopy and UX writing alternatives (headlines, section intros, body microcopy)
current_flagged_state: The current EnsureCollege/ScholarMatch site copy shows the classic 2024-2026 'AI writing tells': (1) frequent em-dashes used as an all-purpose connective tissue between clauses instead of periods, commas, or colons; (2) rule-of-three triads in nearly every headline and section intro (three adjectives, three noun phrases, three short clauses in a row), which reads rhythmically 'generated' rather than composed; (3) reliance on the power verb 'unlock' (and its cousins empower/elevate/unleash) as the default action verb for describing what the product does for the user; (4) 'More than X' / 'not just X, but Y' false-contrast constructions used to introduce a claim by first knocking down a straw-man lesser version of it. Individually each device is a legitimate rhetorical tool; the tell is the frequency and uniformity - the same three patterns recurring across every headline and paragraph, which is not how a single human editor writes across an entire site.
goal: Rewrite headlines, section intros, and microcopy so they read like they were written by one specific, trustworthy person who knows the subject (financial aid, college admissions, scholarships) rather than optimized by a template. The target register is calm, plain, and specific - closer to how Stripe, GOV.UK, Mailchimp, or a good financial-aid counselor would write - varying sentence rhythm naturally, replacing decorative triads and power verbs with concrete nouns/numbers/actions, and stating differentiators directly instead of through negation ('not just X, but Y'). The copy should still feel warm and human-facing (this is a stressed-out students-and-parents audience), but warmth should come from clarity and specificity, not from hype vocabulary.

options.description: Apply the GOV.UK Content Design and Nielsen Norman Group 'writing for the web' method: one idea per sentence, active voice, concrete verbs, no more than ~20-25 words per sentence average, write like you're talking to one user 1:1, target a US grade 7-9 reading level. Cut throat-clearing openers and let the sentence lead with the actual information (inverted pyramid: conclusion first).
options.why_it_reads_human: Real subject-matter experts writing quickly for a real reader default to plain, front-loaded sentences. AI-generated marketing copy over-indexes on ornamental structure (triads, dashes) precisely because it is optimizing for 'sounding polished' rather than for transmitting one piece of information per sentence. Removing the ornamentation and shortening sentences removes the tell by removing the motive for it.
options.adoption_examples: GOV.UK content style guide (mandatory plain English across all pages); ONS (Office for National Statistics) service manual plain-language guidance; Mailchimp Content Style Guide ('plainspoken... strip away hyperbolic language and over-promises')

options.option: Specificity-first copy (numbers, named details, concrete nouns instead of adjectives/triads)
options.description: Replace adjective stacks and rule-of-three abstractions with one concrete, checkable detail: a number, a named scholarship, a deadline, a dollar figure, a named user type. Instead of 'personalized, powerful, and simple matching' say '400+ scholarships filtered to the 12 you actually qualify for.' This is the fintech/B2B SaaS pattern of leading with a specific capability + a specific buyer + a credibility signal, rather than three abstract virtues.
options.why_it_reads_human: Specific, falsifiable claims are what a person with real knowledge of the product writes, because they know the actual numbers; a generic triad of virtues is what gets written when the author doesn't have (or isn't using) a specific fact to reach for. Specificity is also structurally incompatible with the rule-of-three tell, since real numbers rarely arrive in matched sets of three.
options.adoption_examples: Stripe landing-page copy guidance (Stripe Atlas 'Writing copy for landing pages'); fintech copywriting practice of stating exact metrics ('reduces failure rate from 3% to under 1%') instead of adjectives; Ahrefs marketing voice (leads with specific numbers, undercuts its own claims)

options.option: Contextual voice-and-tone system (Mailchimp model) instead of one uniform 'hype' register
options.description: Define a voice (consistent traits: plainspoken, genuine, a little dry) that stays fixed, but let tone flex by context - reassuring and calm on a deadline/error page, matter-of-fact on a data table, slightly warmer on a success/match page. This replaces the current approach of applying the same elevated 'unlock your future' register everywhere regardless of what the user is actually doing on that page.
options.why_it_reads_human: Humans modulate tone based on the reader's situation; a stressed high-schooler checking a deadline needs different copy than one who just got matched to a scholarship. Uniform hype-register copy across every context is itself a tell, because no single human tone fits every page equally well.
options.adoption_examples: Mailchimp Content Style Guide (voice stays constant, tone adjusts to user's emotional state); Shopify Polaris content guidelines ('read it out loud, ask if a human would say it'); Apple Human Interface Guidelines writing principles (context-appropriate brevity)

options.option: Sentence-rhythm variance edit (kill the triad/em-dash tic through deliberate cadence mixing)
options.description: A line-edit pass whose only job is rhythm: for every headline/section intro, check for (a) a rule-of-three list - cut to one or two items or expand to four with an 'and'/', plus' break; (b) an em-dash - replace roughly 80% of them with a period, comma, or colon depending on the logical relationship; keep at most one em-dash per page for genuine emphasis. Deliberately mix short (under 8 word) and longer (20+ word) sentences instead of medium-length uniform sentences.
options.why_it_reads_human: AI text-generation is locally fluent but globally repetitive - it reuses a favorite structure across a whole document because each individual instance still looks fine in isolation. Human editors self-monitor for repetition across a document, which produces uneven, varied rhythm. A budget/quota approach (e.g., 'no page gets more than one triad and one em-dash') mechanically reproduces that human variance.
options.adoption_examples: Widely discussed 2025 'signs of AI writing' analyses (Wikipedia's own 'Signs of AI writing' style guidance flags exactly this pattern for editors); style critiques of em-dash/triad overuse in AI-generated marketing copy

options.option: Verb audit: retire 'unlock/empower/elevate/unleash' for plain, action-tied verbs
options.description: Build a short banned-word list (unlock, empower, elevate, unleash, seamless, leverage, streamline, game-changing, cutting-edge) and replace each instance with the actual verb for what the user does or gets: find, match, apply, save, compare, track, qualify for, submit, get matched to. The replacement verb should be testable against 'what does the user literally click or receive.'
options.why_it_reads_human: Power verbs are abstract stand-ins used when the writer wants to imply transformation without specifying the mechanism. A person who actually knows the product writes the mechanism ('we check your GPA and zip code against 400 scholarship rules') rather than the abstraction ('unlock your potential').
options.adoption_examples: 2025-2026 copywriting critiques of 'unlock/empower' as overused AI-coded marketing words (Jenny Lucas Copywriting 'Annoying words and phrases killing your marketing copy in 2026'); WordStream/CrazyEgg power-word lists explicitly called out as now-diluted

options.option: Direct-differentiation statements instead of 'More than X' / 'not just X, but Y' contrastive negation
options.description: Replace the pattern of naming a strawman first ('This isn't just a scholarship search - it's a partner in your journey') with a direct, positive statement of what the thing is, then (if a contrast is truly useful) make it once, specifically, and later in the copy rather than as the opening move.
options.why_it_reads_human: This construction ('not X, it's Y') is one of the most-cited 2025-2026 AI tells because it manufactures a sense of insight/depth cheaply - it sounds like a reveal without requiring new information. Real writers only reach for negation when there's a genuine, specific misconception to correct; using it as a default sentence-opening device is the tell.
options.adoption_examples: Multiple 2025 AI-detection style analyses name 'contrastive negation' / 'negative parallelism' explicitly (GC.ai 'AI Writing Pattern to Know: Contrastive Negation'; refine.so 'Not just X, but Y: the AI pattern'); style advice: 'lead with what the thing is, delete the defensive framing'

recommended: Combine three of the above into one lightweight editorial pass, in this order of priority: (1) Specificity-first copy as the primary fix - because EnsureCollege has real numbers to use (scholarship counts, dollar amounts, deadlines, match percentages), leading with those instead of adjective triads solves the rule-of-three and power-verb problems at once, since a real number rarely arrives in a matched set of three and doesn't need 'unlock' to sound impactful; (2) the sentence-rhythm/em-dash budget as a mechanical guardrail applied during copy review (max one em-dash and one three-item list per page); (3) the verb audit as a find-and-replace pass against a small banned-word list. Skip the full Mailchimp-style contextual voice system for now - it's the right long-term investment but is over-engineering for a single-person editorial site; a one-page voice note ('plainspoken, specific, a little dry, no hype verbs') captures 80% of the value at a fraction of the effort. This combination fits the site's existing character: Fraunces (an editorial serif) and Space Mono (a technical/data mono) already signal 'considered, slightly technical, not corporate-hype' - so the copy should match that register with plain declarative sentences and real numbers set in the mono face, rather than aspirational marketing language. It also matches the owner's dashboard-leaning preference: dashboards are built on specific data points, not adjectives, so specificity-first copy is the natural extension of that visual sensibility into the writing.
trend_context: 2025-2026 has produced a specific, well-documented backlash against a handful of ChatGPT-era stylistic tics - em-dash overuse, rule-of-three/tricolon padding, contrastive negation ('it's not X, it's Y'), and a shortlist of power verbs (unlock, empower, elevate, unleash, leverage, seamless, cutting-edge) that are now widely cited as 'AI tells' rather than persuasive devices, precisely because they have become so uniform across AI-generated marketing content that their overuse itself signals authorlessness. In parallel, the UX-writing field's actual trajectory (NN/g, GOV.UK, Mailchimp, Shopify Polaris) has been toward plain, scannable, specific, context-modulated copy for a decade, and AI-generated first-draft copy is now the industry's main tool for producing exactly the kind of undifferentiated 'marketese' that NN/g research already showed users distrust and skim past (concise + scannable + objective copy measured 124% better usability than typical promotional copy). What reads current in 2025-2026: short declarative sentences, one concrete number or named detail per claim, restrained/dry tone that treats the absence of hype as a credibility signal (the Stripe/Ahrefs pattern - 'if we won't exaggerate in the marketing, we probably won't exaggerate about the product'), and voice systems that flex tone contextually. What reads dated or AI-templated: uniform 'inspirational' registers, decorative triads, em-dash-linked clauses, and defensive not-X-but-Y openers used as a stylistic default rather than a deliberate, occasional device.
vanilla_css_notes: This is primarily a content/editorial change (no new components), so implementation is mostly HTML text edits, but a few things are worth building deliberately: (1) Set a comfortable measure for editorial copy - `max-inline-size: 65ch` (or `60ch` for Fraunces headlines) on paragraph/heading containers so plain, longer declarative sentences (which this rewrite favors over choppy triads) don't sprawl edge-to-edge. (2) Reserve genuine em-dash emphasis, when kept, as `<strong>`/color-accent (marigold) rather than punctuation-only emphasis - e.g. wrap the one real number/claim per section in a `.stat` span styled with the marigold accent and Space Mono, so specificity is visually reinforced instead of relying on em-dash cadence to signal importance. (3) Because CSP is `script-src 'self'` (no inline scripts), any automated enforcement of the style rules (banned-word list, em-dash/triad budget) must live in a build-time lint step, not client-side JS - e.g. a small Node script (`scripts/copy-lint.js`) run in CI or pre-commit that greps built HTML/Markdown source for banned words (unlock, empower, elevate, unleash, seamless, leverage) and flags files with more than one em-dash or one three-item comma list per section; this catches regressions without adding any runtime JS to the page at all. (4) If an 'eyebrow' label or section kicker is used to replace a triad-headline pattern (e.g. a small Space Mono all-caps label above a plain Fraunces headline), style it with `letter-spacing` and `font-variant: small-caps` or `text-transform: uppercase` at a small size - this gives visual rhythm/hierarchy that used to be manufactured by em-dashes and triads, without needing the punctuation trick.
accessibility_notes: Plain, shorter sentences and a lower reading-level target (roughly US grade 7-9, per GOV.UK/ONS guidance) directly benefit users with cognitive disabilities, non-native English speakers, and screen-reader users, and are explicitly called out in WCAG's plain-language supporting guidance. Reducing em-dash frequency also has a concrete screen-reader benefit: some screen readers announce or pause oddly around em-dashes when overused mid-sentence, and dense triad lists inside a single sentence (rather than a marked-up `<ul>`) are harder for screen-reader users to parse than an actual list element - if a genuine three-item enumeration is kept, mark it up as a real `<ul>`/`<ol>` rather than a comma-joined prose triad. Maintain heading hierarchy (one `<h1>`, sequential `<h2>`/`<h3>`) so the plainer, shorter headlines this rewrite produces still give screen-reader users a usable page outline. Ensure the marigold accent color used for 'specificity' callouts (stat spans, eyebrow labels) meets WCAG AA contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text) against the light background - test the exact marigold hex at both text and label sizes, since accent colors used for small caps/mono labels often sit right at the contrast boundary.
pitfalls: Specificity-first copy can curdle into 'specificity theater' - fake-precise numbers ('87% more likely to qualify') invented to sound rigorous rather than measured; only use numbers that are actually true and sourced from the product's real data.

Plain-language/GOV.UK-style rewrites, taken too far, can read cold and institutional for a brand that needs to feel hopeful and supportive to stressed students and parents - plainness should remove ornamentation, not warmth; keep second-person address and the occasional encouraging aside.

The verb audit can just swap one now-cliche verb for the next trendy one (e.g. replacing 'unlock' with 'unstuck', 'navigate', or 'level up') - the fix only works if the replacement verb is tied to a literal, checkable user action, not another abstraction.

Overcorrecting the rhythm edit can produce uniformly short, choppy staccato sentences that become their own tell (the 'punchy AI listicle' voice) - the goal is variance, not uniform brevity; deliberately keep some longer, information-dense sentences alongside short ones.

Removing 'not just X, but Y' entirely can remove a genuinely useful rhetorical tool - the fix is frequency and placement (use it rarely, only for a real misconception, and not as the opening move of a section), not a hard ban.

A one-page voice note (the low-effort version of Option 3) can drift out of sync with actual practice if no one re-reads new copy against it before publishing - even a lightweight system needs a two-minute self-check step ('does this sound like our one-page note?') built into whatever process adds new copy to the site.
